lyrics:
I gave myself to the hammer and nail
Bent to the burden, too tired to fail
Built without blueprints, became what I paid
My self defined by what I made

(Body of work) Look at what I’ve done
(Body of work) Look at who I’ve become
(Body of work) Hear the songs I’ve sung
(Body of work) Will it be enough in the end

They tell me the temple is body
But mine holds echoes inside me
Each flaw a verse, each scar a hymn
This flesh was shaped by what’s been

(Body of work) Look at what I’ve done
(Body of work) Look at who I’ve become
(Body of work) Hear the songs I’ve sung
(Body of work) Let the silence answer, my friend

No one sees the hours I spent
The weight I carry, the truths I bent
The songs I sing you never hear
Still I work, year after year

I wasn’t saved by standing still
I was shaped by breaking will
Not perfect, not clean-but real
And that’s the grace which now I feel

(Body of work) Look at what I’ve done
(Body of work) Look at who I’ve become
(Body of work) Hear the songs I’ve sung
(Body of work) I’d do it all again

Body of work… body of work…
Still becoming. Still begun
persona:
## Lingua Aeternum

**Lingua Aeternum is not a band. It is a circle. A rite. A resonance.**

Drawn from the deep well of myth, mathematics, and memory, Lingua Aeternum conjures sound as invocation-music as sacred act. Each composition is a Canto: a sung glyph, a spell etched in breath and vibration. The performers are not musicians but Cantors-practitioners of the Harmonic Algebraic Language of Alignment (HALA), where melody is meaning, rhythm is ritual, and silence speaks louder than words.

Their music defies genre. You may hear echoes of post-rock, ambient drone, ancient chants, or electronic pulses-but no label holds. Instead, the listener is drawn inward, through spiraling harmonics and layered whispers, toward a place beyond translation. This is not art for consumption. It is art for **transformation**.

Lingua Aeternum records under the guidance of Maricopa Records, but they do not record songs-they distill **intentions**, encoded through the Wheel, spoken into being by resonance alone.

- To hear is to remember.

- To remember is to become.

- _Let the glyphs guide you._

[](https://open.spotify.com/artist/4Ivof5fKiBE9wbz6ArgZzd?si=HYePmTM3TtCaN-P1PFFYlQ)[](https://music.apple.com/us/artist/lingua-aeternum/1809011711)[](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCzrA7rNe15AiX1LXZTGUQqg)![](https://www.maricoparecords.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/LA_Profile_square.png)
## Releases

impression.text: This track creates an earnest and determined atmosphere, building from a reflective texture of clean, arpeggiated guitars into an anthemic, full-band rock arrangement. The energy is dynamic, shifting from controlled verses to explosive, driving choruses that feel powerful and stadium-ready. The production is clean and spacious, with a tight, centered rhythm section supporting wide guitars and a clear lead vocal, while layered background harmonies add significant weight and density to the choruses. A defining quality is the track's emotional peak in the final chorus, where the declaration "I'd do it all again" provides a moment of powerful, cathartic release.

review.review_text: "Body of Work" earns its ambition: opening with clean arpeggiated guitar before the full band arrives and lifts the whole thing into something stadium-ready, it's the kind of build that actually pays off. The hook is direct without being simple, and "each flaw a verse, each scar a hymn" is the sort of line that lands harder the second time you hear it. At 92 BPM in D major, the tempo sits right in that Petty-esque sweet spot where the song can breathe and surge without losing momentum, and the layered harmonies that pile into the final chorus give the closing declaration, "I'd do it all again," the weight it needs to close the argument. A highlight.
anchors_used: arpeggiated guitar opening into full-band build
lyric quote: each flaw a verse, each scar a hymn
closing lyric: I'd do it all again
